title: 2025/01/30 - FD [Part 1]
displayTitle: 2025-01-30 - Foreign Affairs, Defence and Trade Committee - 2025/01/30 - FD [Part 1] [Part 1]
committee: Foreign Affairs, Defence and Trade Committee
description: Thursday 30 January 2025 | Foreign Affairs, Defence and Trade Committee

chunks.text: All right. Good morning. Good morning. I'll see you again, Victoria and Luke. Yes, yes. Great to have you along with us today for, uh, the start of our consideration of the agreement under the United Nations Convention Law of Sea and the conservation of sustainable use of marine, biological diversity of areas beyond national jurisdiction. And we'll just go with BB and J for that. Yes. No.

So thanks for coming along today. We look forward to your, um, your information, your introduction comments on that. We have half an hour set down for consideration of that. Um, we've received the National interest analysis, uh, and your commentary alongside that.

I welcome your comments, and then I'm sure there'll be views from members as well. Thank you. Well, well, good morning to the committee. Um, as you've commented, uh, Mr. Chair, you already have all the relevant, um, paperwork. So I thought I would just, um, outline a few key points and then answer questions.

Uh, the bb and j agreement is a key implementing agreement under the UN Convention on the Law of the Sea, uh, which is a really important international agreement for New Zealand. Um, the bb and j agreement was adopted by consensus, which is quite a remarkable achievement in the current, um, international negotiating environment.

And it was done so after many years of complex negotiations with the most intense period of negotiations taking place between 2018 and 2023. Uh, the agreement fills gaps in the unclos system, uh, and by providing the tools and systems necessary to protect high seas by diversity in areas beyond national jurisdiction.

And by that, uh, we mean, uh, the high seas, uh, beyond, uh, exclusive economic zones and what is called the area with a capital A, uh, which is the seabed beyond the end of the continental shelf. In New Zealand's case, uh, over a hundred, uh, states have signed the agreement, um, and, uh, including many of our closest partners.

There are currently 15 states, uh, which have ratified and a large number of others that are well progressed in their ratification process. The agreement is expected, uh, to enter into force in early, uh, 2026. Um, the overall objective of the agreement is to ensure conservation and sustainable use of marine biological, uh, diversity, uh, in these areas beyond national jurisdiction through effective implementation of Unclos, the UN Convention of the Law of the Sea, and, and further international cooperation and coordination.

Um, I won't go into the four substantive parts that form the backbone of the agreement because I think that though they are clearly, uh, set out, uh, in the, uh, documents you had before you, but just to, I would like to emphasize, um, that becoming party to the agreement supports a number of significant strategic legal, economic and environmental interests for New Zealand.

And these interests include upholding the central centrality of the UN Convention on the Law of the Sea and strengthening its governance framework, uh, New Zealand maintaining influence on ocean's issues of importance to our region and to the international rules-based order contributing to vital efforts to, uh, protect marine biodiversity and promoting greater cooperation in global marine science research and innovation.

Um, and one of the key advantages for New Zealand ratifying the agreement in a timely manner is our ability to ensure that future decisions taken by the mechanisms set up under the BB and j agreement will advance our national interests. And that includes influencing how this agreement, um, and interacts with other ocean governance frameworks of importance to us, uh, particularly the interaction between this agreement and regional fisheries management organizations and this agreement and the Antarctic treaty system,

which I know is well known, uh, to this committee. Um, in order to fulfill the obligations under the agreement, uh, there will be a need for implementing legislation. Um, and there will also be a need to develop some new regulatory tools to ensure New Zealand remains compliant with the notification reporting and other requirements of the agreement.

Uh, there are some costs associated, uh, with joining the agreement, uh, but these are expected, uh, to be small, uh, given that are, it only applies to areas beyond national jurisdiction. Uh, New Zealand could ratify the agreement once the parliamentary treaty examination process has been completed, uh, and implementing legislation has passed.

Um, now the National Interest Analysis, uh, goes into this in, uh, many, uh, in more detail. Uh, but we are very happy to answer questions, um, from the committee. Excellent. Alright. Thank you for your time. Have we got questions from you? But this, Uh, thank you, Mr. Chair and I also find it, we in kind of this a very interesting and unique situation where we have the party who's negotiating and also drafting the NIA and also a submitter, um, which I think is unlike any other processes that we do see.

Um, my question is around the interaction between this particular agreement and the global Ocean stream, and how is that also moving towards protecting 30% of our ocean? Uh, well, um, the chair, uh, said that we would call it the BB and J agreement.

Uh, and it is indeed, uh, the most common, uh, descriptor for it. The treaty is also informally called the High Seas Biodiversity Treaty and, um, by some the Global Oceans Treaty. So it is indeed one and the same, uh, is my understanding. Um, and in that case, uh, please correct me if I'm wrong, a supplementary question, Mr.

Chair. Um, just going through the national interest analysis, going through the NIA and I may have missed it, but if I have, could you point me out? Where does the NIA actually that's specify or state the 30% protection, The, um, campaign for 30% protection of, um, the high seas, uh, is not a explicit part of the agreement.

Uh, so we are not making particular commitments under the agreement. However, the agreement does provide the future framework, which will enable the establishment of area-based management tools and NPAs, so is considered to be a vehicle for achieving, uh, those objectives by many.

One last possibly, but last supplementary question. So, um, again, um, apologies if I misunderstood this and happy for you to clarify, but my understanding was a big part and a big sort of area was celebrated under the, the formerly known Global Oceans Treaty, and now, um, BNJ was around the 30% protection.

But what you're saying now is that that is not explicitly part of the BB NJ, and that is just now BB NJ is a tool to sort of move towards that. Doesn't that sort of, in that case, nickel negates what was originally a very core part of the Global Ocean Street in terms of that, that level of, you know, that blanket protection, The agreement provides the tools to protect marine biodiversity on the high seas?

Yeah, it doesn't con include any specific targets or percentages, but it is, uh, absolutely widely considered to be the vehicle that provides the tools and mechanisms to achieve, uh, aims around marine biodiversity. Do you know of any other countries, uh, that are party to the agreement, um, that has made that commitment to protect, uh, 30% of the ocean or above 30% of that ocean?

I'm aware that a number of countries have made, uh, statements around their support, uh, for that. And if we ratify this particular agreement, is that something that MFA is gonna be moving towards as the next step of this? Like you mentioned, Following the rat ratification of the agreement, if that is what occurs, uh, we would see it as a priority for New Zealand to play a significant role in all the mechanisms established under the treaty.

Uh, and we would see that as being important for achieving, um, achieving the objectives of the treaty, but would obviously also, uh, be guided by the government of the day on the priorities on specific issues. Thank you so much. One more. Damien O'Connor. Uh, thanks. Do you think this will make any real difference to IUU phishing?

The agreement doesn't directly, um, deal with phishing. In fact, uh, the agreement requires, uh, a respect for the competencies of existing regional fisheries management organizations. Uh, so that is not explicitly its purpose. It does set up a framework where, uh, there should be more engagement and cooperation between the different, um, organizations that are responsible for activities on the high seas.

And that includes regional fisheries management organizations. Uh, if a area based management tool is adopted by the agreement and the high seas, uh, that indeed, uh, would have competence over and, um, would have an impact on fishing in that area. But that would only happen if there was not already an existing regional fisheries management organization in place for the relevant area.
Do you think this just might set up another parking space for debate that is critical regarding marine biodiversity? Instead of actually grappling with the real issues around over exploitation?

The effectiveness of the agreement will depend on all the parties, uh, engaging in good faith, uh, and pursuing the objectives of the treaty, which are indeed to protect marine biodiversity. Uh, so it's hard to protect in advance how effective that will be because it depends on all the members.

Uh, but I do see, uh, the, as a very significant and positive development in the, um, UNCLOS framework. Would it be fair to say that? Yeah, I mean, you've already shared with us that it's not focused solely around fisheries management. It's actually around a number of other aspects in relation to modernization, I suppose, or a better grasping of the increased reach or technology that's available.

Um, and one thing I guess that stood out for me was the unknown volume. You know, we're still talking potentially a couple of million odd unknown species out there that haven't yet been discovered. So having some rules around how we might deal with those sort of things seems like a reasonable step. And I think, yeah, I guess the question is, you know, for New Zealand being involved, is that a natural fit with what you would see from our traditional perspective environmentally?

From an notion's perspective? Uh, I would agree, Mr. Chair, that the focus of the agreement is more on the vast array of, um, to a large extent unknown and un understood marine biodiversity, much of which I understand is, uh, uh, is very tiny and, um, uh, wrote needs a, a subject on which, um, scientific knowledge needs to develop rather than on the more tangible marine biodiversity that is already being exploited.

So I would certainly agree with you. Um, New Zealand has always, uh, over decades played paid co close attention to, uh, international law. Regarding oceans, uh, oceans are extremely important, uh, for us for, for many reasons. Uh, the UN convention on the Law of the Sea, uh, is the treaty that guarantees to us our large maritime domain.

And, uh, the economic resources that come with that. The rules of the sea are also extremely important, uh, that they work smoothly because, um, most of the large part, part of our goods trade is transported, uh, via ocean, via oceans. Uh, so at the higher strategic level, um, it's a very important area.

We can be heavily impacted by, uh, shipping accidents. Uh, the environment, uh, is, uh, fragile. Uh, so participating in this treaty, uh, is an extremely, uh, natural, uh, thing for a country like New Zealand to do. Given our long history, uh, dating back to the early days of the negotiation of Unclos, Can you just give us a bit more insight into how you feel the treaty will provide additional influence in relation to the Antarctic treaty system?

A key principle of the treaty is that it, uh, must respect the existing competencies of other, uh, sectoral, uh, and regional bodies. Uh, so the Antarctic treaty system, uh, as, um, this committee knows, has a even longer history than the law of the SEA Convention.

It's, uh, played a, played a very important role since 1959. Um, so we would see it as important that the two systems, the bb and j system and the Antarctic Treaty system are cognizant, uh, of what is going on in each domain. Uh, but we also think it's important that the Antarctic Treaty system continues to play its pay its role, um, and would for the most part, uh, think that the BB and j uh, competence, uh, would end where the Antarctic treaty system ums competence begins.

Can you provide an update on the discussions with LAU as to whether or not New Zealand's ratification will extend to them? Mm-hmm. Question. I think we would've to provide that update in writing. Um, I'm not sure that we have, um, yet that confirmation, um, but certainly those consultations have been taking place. Okay.

Yeah, it would be useful to have an update on that. Uh, honorable Dam O'Connor. Thank you. Uh, CB mining, was it part of the early discussions? Indeed. Do you think this agreement should cover Seabed mining? Seabed mining in areas beyond national jurisdiction is regulated by the International Seabed Authority, uh, which is another branch of the Unclos family of, um, organizations.

Uh, this agreement, um, will need to respect again, the competence of the International CBE Authority, but it does bring in, um, some clear, uh, I guess, clear understandings around the importance of marine biodiversity and, um, some additional, additional tools.

And the two organizations, the mechanisms established under the BB and j will need to work with the International CBE Authority. And of course, we are the same members, uh, largely in both, in both organizations. So if the country's taken their full set of obligations into each, uh, each process.

Uh, Mr. Chairman, just again, to, to to guess, to go back to my point about just having another, um, uh, parking space, if not cul-de-sac with some of these discussions, do you think that, um, control of sea mining and, um, aspirations around, um, oceans biodiversity can be disconnected in any way?

Well, the great advantage in, uh, pursuing the protection of marine biodiversity under the Unclos framework is that it does indeed all remain, uh, connected. And that's of, uh, considerable strategic importance for New Zealand. Uh, we want the Unclos regime to, to remain strong and effective, and we, uh, tend to look on solutions being developed outside the Unclos regime as, uh, as damaging to that and damaging to the way in which Uncross has, um, endured, uh, for many decades as a kind of constitution for the oceans.

Do you think that the principles of, um, of that management outside jurisdiction or inside the jurisdiction, do you think that those principles should be consistent? I think to a large extent they are already, and you'll find, uh, provisions of the bb and j agreement and provisions of the way in which the International Sea B Authority is operating, uh, uh, fundamentally consistent as they derive from the same treaty framework. Dr. Lauren, Thank you so much. Um, I'm still trying to, um, sort

of see if you wouldn't mind helping us, uh, elucidate the idea of something, a tangible action that is resulting from us enacting this particular agreement. So, so far from what we're hearing is that, you know, it doesn't really directly ban or looking at, you know, address the issue of IEU, um, that the On Damia economy mentioned.

It doesn't specifically mention anything about protection ocean of oceans, which was under the intentions of the Global Oceans Treaty. Um, can I just check one of the, some of the other submitters also mentions other destructive fishing practices that does happen on High Sea, such as bottom trolling. Does this agreement, at least as a move towards us addressing these, some of these other destructive, uh, practices.

And, you know, and on the honorable, Daniel Con also mentioned seabed mining as well. So I'm just trying to figure out what is something that is tangible that we can talk about? One of the, um, primary ways that the treaty is expected to protect marine biodiversity is through the establishment of area-based management tools, uh, particularly marine protected areas.

Up until now, there hasn't been a global system for establishing marine protected areas in the high seas. Uh, this is the treaty that establishes, uh, that potential and that is seen to be, uh, one of the, uh, most tangible and positive ways that the treaty will contribute to the same.

And I expect that once the treaty enters into force, there will be a, uh, big movement of cooperation to try and prove the treaty's worth by establishing some of these marine protected areas, uh, in a way that they make a difference. Okay. So, um, sorry, You costley, just a supplementary on that one.

Would that, would that you got Yeah, yeah. So on the NPAs, would that be the kind of comidex proposal that that has been talked about? Although that's The MPAs, uh, would be in areas beyond national jurisdiction. So, uh, quite different from, uh, the example you've cited.

Um, it would potentially be a more similar to, I actually, I, I'm not sure. Are there other, You're talking a whole, like a Pacific Forum though, right? You're talking sort of quite big picture. Yeah, big picture. They would need to, um, be adopted under the, the mechanisms of the new treaty.

So there are decision making processes. There are, uh, guidelines around, uh, what an MPA should cover, you know, how it should work, and they will be further evolved through the mechanisms of the treaty. But that is one of the, you know, the key, uh, new exciting areas that, um, this treaty, uh, will achieve, I guess. Sorry, supplementary that you just follow from what I was mentioning before.

You mentioned in terms of this sort of new global body and that's being created as a result that has potential being created as a result of this. What sort of, for lack of better words, what, what sort of teeth would this body have in addressing some of these? Because we've seen, we do see a lot of international, um, bodies being created, but it's come to geopolitics.

It's, uh, at risk of, you know, countries pulling out, pulling, being pulled in. Um, you know, us recently is talking about pulling out of the Paris agreement. So what then tangible teeth would this have in holding things like IUU, things like bottom trolling, things like seabed mining, mining accountable.

The UN Convention on the Law of the Sea, uh, has, uh, quite a, an effective and strong dispute settlement system. So the core obligations are legally binding on members. Uh, this is also a binding treaty. Uh, so has this the same, the same force.

Um, and, uh, there's every reason to think, uh, that it will be effective. There's a, there's a large, um, common interest in protecting marine biodiversity. Next question. And that was evidenced by the fact that, uh, although the negotiations were quite protracted and complex, in the end, it was adopted by consensus and all the countries in the world were in the room in there.

Very good. Tim Costley. Yeah, I'll, I'll, we sort of touched on, so my mom was gonna be around that sort of the teeth in it, and if the and costs mechanisms are quite that, that's what you just said they do. So let me just try and wrap my mind together. I'll, I'll break it down to a bit of an elevator pitch for the, for the average kiwi, like me, uh, very average, um, this effectively is about establishing forums, particularly regionally based forums like in the Pacific, focused on bio sustainability of that bio, those areas of biodiversity beyond national jurisdictions.

And it seeks to do that through cooperation and consultation that is, and, and the extent that the law of the seas mechanisms apply to how that's implemented. Enforced. What, what am I missing there? I think That's partly right, but actually this is a global forum for mechanisms to be adopted for tools and mechanisms to be adopted to protect marine biodiversity at a global level, yes, but the mechanisms may be regional, but the key thing is if they ag are agreed on at a global level, there is a wider buy-in, uh, given

that the areas covered by the treaty Yeah. Are, um, crossed and used by a great many, um, from many countries. Yeah. When you talk Pacific, great Atlantic, there's huge areas. Yeah. Um, uh, but there aren't specific tools yet. This is establishing the forum and, and the pathway to that, or are there specific tools that come in on day one that say you're gonna have to go in?

No, this is establishing the mechanisms Yes. To create these tools. There are some obligations that would come in on day one, uh, in the area of environmental impact assessments. Uh, the Law of the SEA Treaty contains some broad obligations on environmental impact assessments, but they have never been, uh, made operational or well articulated.

The treaty does that and will require, um, a higher standard of environmental impact assessment before activities are conducted in the high seas. Because I think there's some, some of, to some of, um, Dr. Nan's points, you know, that general interest from the public. Yeah. How are we actually gonna stop seabed mining?

How are we gonna stop some other, um, uh, actor coming in, you know, taking resources that might not be in our national jurisdiction, but just over the border of it. And, and, and so yeah. But there's, there's not, we're not at that stage yet, are we? This is setting up the mechanism, which is gonna apply globally, but which will be eventually operationalized in our, in our area of Interest. Yes. With a focus on marine biodiversity, uh, rather than some of the areas which are well regulated.

Well, resource shipping is well regulated, fishing is quite well regulated, but there are gaps. You know, the whole globe is not covered by regional fishery management organizations by any means. Thank you. That's, That's really helpful. All right, next question, Laura. I'm clear. Thank You. And Simon kind of follows on from terms, and it's a little bit about the infrastructure of what it will look like from day one.

So do we, from our end, do you have, um, experts appointed that will be representing us, and how does that actually look like? So we have this framework in place. Have you got the resource, the expert, and done that part of the work behind it to be able to come to that table, um, and maybe potentially take a lead on some of these things from a New Zealand perspective?

That's a really good question. Um, the first stage, uh, between now and entry into force, and probably for a short period of time after entry into force mm-hmm. Is on establishing the, um, the structure, the, and the committees and the processes that will operate, uh, the treaty sets out, uh, how these will work.

But they still need to be adopted and be given terms of reference. Uh, I think, um, we are well resourced, uh, to participate in that. The team that engaged in the negotiations, uh, which I led, uh, had participation from the ministry for primary industries, from the Department of Conservation, um, the main ones, Uh, in terms of Ministry for Environment at, at times, uh, so we are well coordinated across government.

Uh, we had input, uh, from a wider range of organizations, uh, such as, um, it's hard to envisage exactly what the human resourcing requirements would be for attendance at the processes, um, once they're up and running. Uh, and I would imagine our engagement, uh, would on specific issues would depend a little bit on the, um, on the importance at the time of that issue.

So if we were working, for example, with our Pacific Island, uh, partners on establishing something in the Pacific Ocean, uh, you can imagine that would require quite, quite a focus for a while. Uh, we might be, uh, watching, but less, uh, sort of hands-on if something was being developed in the North Atlantic, but obviously it would be important to, uh, participate and understand.

Very good. Um, I always get a little nervous when I see the words, there will be some costs associated with this. Um, can you give us some insight into what sort of number that may be? Obviously, notwithstanding the comments you've just made about variability of resources for different pieces, but have, have you got some insight hopefully around the anticipated costs?

Yes. The, um, the costs, I guess, fall into several different buckets. Uh, like any, uh, treaty regime that establishes, uh, mechanisms and a bit of architecture, uh, there are costs to join the treaty and an annual subscription fee. Uh, what that depends on what, what that amounts to will depend on a number of factors, uh, including the number of parties, uh, at the time, uh, the treaty enters into force.

Uh, but we do have some indicative figures, which I think are in the NIA, can you help me with those on what costs of similar organizations are like, uh, we are talking about costs that in other organizations such as the Antarctic Treaty system, the International Seabed Authority range from kind of 40,000 to New Zealand per annum through to the low hundreds.

I think, um, there is a additional, um, cost for the bb and j agreement, which is whatever our subscription annual subscription is, uh, as a developed country, we will also make an additional 50% of that into a pool to support, uh, the capacity for developing countries to be able to fully implement the agreement.

Uh, and that's, um, was something that was debated at length in the negotiations. And one of the areas where environmental agreements have been seen at times to fall short is that, uh, some of the developing countries lack the capacity to implement. So this is a treaty that is well set up from the start with inbuilt funding, uh, to support that.

And given, uh, the priority that our Pacific partners have placed on this agreement and the importance for them of marine biodiversity, uh, that's a very positive factor. So you have, uh, fairly minor costs of an on an annual basis. Uh, then, um, there will also need to be, um, mechanisms for us to comply with our reporting requirements.

Uh, so some additional impost on government, but relatively minor and aligned with the types of things governments departments are doing already. And then private actors who are wanting to conduct activities on the high seas, uh, will need to engage to a certain extent in those reporting requirements.

If, for example, niwa was collecting, um, marine biodiversity on the high seas as part of their research, uh, they will have, um, some reporting requirements, uh, on them, on them. Uh, but, uh, it's not considered a high cost Treaty, uh, for New Zealand to be part of.

Alright. Look, we've run over time, so we'll call it there. But thank you very much Victoria and Luke for your insights today. Appreciate it. Thank you for the, um, good questions. Yes. Okay. We'll move now to our next submitter, uh, the environment and conservation organizations of New Zealand Incorporated, who will be joining us via Zoom.
Are they online? They are. Excellent.

All right. I think we have Barry Weber co-chair joining us. Can you hear us, Barry? I can indeed. Tato. Um, Excellent. All right. Welcome along. We have seen your submission. Uh, we have 10 minutes set down to hear from you, and I'm sure members may have questions as well over to you.

Um, thank you again. Um, and thank you for this opportunity to make a submission and support of New Zealand ratifying the, uh, BB and J agreement. Um, this is an important, uh, uh, agreement. It's probably the most important, uh, marine treaty that's been negotiated in the last 30 years. It's, uh, it was, uh, carefully, uh, I suppose a, a carefully negotiated treaty given the number of interests around the planet in different aspects of it.

And, um, um, I would acknowledge the work that, uh, foreign affairs and others have put into, uh, negotiating on New Zealand's behalf. And, um, I was part of the New Zealand delegation, put a number of the negotiations, whether online, um, or in person. Um, so my, my comments are basically in support of the treaty, uh, just acknowledging there's four elements of it.

The first element that deals with governing of, um, marine genetic genetic resources on the high seas. There's currently no, uh, current arrangement to do that in terms of managing, uh, biodiversity. And it's a, and it's a key element. Um, the, the other el elements of it is, uh, managing, um, area based management tools, including marine protected areas.

Um, as, uh, the foreign affairs, um, reps have said there's no currently no arrangement apart from in the Antarctic, uh, where, uh, uh, marine, uh, areas can be protected. Uh, so there's no, uh, co coherent, uh, management regime on the high seas. And there are also gaps in the, in the system in terms of, uh, regional fisheries management organizations, um, if, if those bodies, if, uh, adopted any compet competency in that area.

And most body, most of the RFMOs don't have competency in terms of marine protection. Um, and the other elements basically is, um, the, the issues in regard to, um, environmental impact assessment, which you've heard about it, and, uh, capacity building and technology sharing. And there's a whole range of mechanisms in the, in the architecture of the agreement, which are really important, uh, including a science and technical body.

Um, there's a clearinghouse mechanism, which is a really important tool for a range of issues in terms of, uh, making it, lowering the cost actually of, uh, understanding, um, both in terms of environmental impact assessment, but also in regard to, um, marine genetic resources and also, um, area based management tools. So that, those are all really important.

Um, some of that architecture that's in this agreement is missing in a number of international agreements. The UN Fish Stocks Agreement is, is one of those which is really missing the more institutional architecture that, um, this agreement has. Um, so this is, that's why it's really important. There's a whole range of other dispute, uh, arrangements which add to the un conventional the Law of the Sea.

Um, and I I would also acknowledge the work, um, that, um, the Pacific Island, uh, countries played in a whole range of parts of the legislation, including the acknowledgement of traditional knowledge in the provisions of the treaty. Um, one of the things I would say, just, um, just responding to one of the questions that, uh, uh, was asked in regard to, um, the protection, the, the, the idea of, um, protecting 30% of the Marines area under the, um, convention on bi, uh, biological diversity.

Um, this arrangement would allow for, um, for marine protected areas to be established on the high seas in areas where they currently c can't be, which isn't basically everything outside of the Antarctic, uh, outside of, um, camel laws waters. That's the convention on the conservation of Antarctic marine living resources.

So that's, that's, um, so it's a really important treaty, uh, by being on the ground floor, um, by ratifying the treaty early, it would allow us, uh, now New Zealand to be involved in that process. Um, there's a range of pre prep comms being established, um, starting in April this year. Um, another one later in the year, and also, uh, one established in, in sometime in, in, um, 2026.

And it's really important that we're part of that, part of that process. And there's clearly a, a whole range of countries who, um, getting involved, um, both in signing the treaty, it's over 107 countries and ratifying the treaty 15 countries have so far ratified, it requires 60 to come into force.

Um, there's a whole initiative going on in the EU at the moment, uh, to ratify, as far as I know, the EU itself has now passed all its things it needs to, to ratify, and it's waiting on a number of its member states to do that. So there's a whole, uh, initiative going on for the un oceans, uh, uh, treaty, uh, sorry, un oceans meeting, um, happening un o happening, um, uh, uh, in the first half of this year.

And a number of countries have made commitments to, uh, ratify the convention prior to that. So open to any questions, and thanks very much for the committee to, uh, listening to, um, and accepting our submission. Thanks. Excellent. Thank you very much for taking the time to submit on this. Do we have questions for Mr. Weber submission?

Yeah, pretty, pretty straightforward. So obviously you're advocating what I pick up for a prompt ratification, uh, and New Zealand, uh, seating that treaty That that's correct. And, um, it will be, uh, well seen in the Pacific, uh, of New Zealand ratifying the treaty given the strong support in the Pacific for, um, for the treaty itself. Thanks.

Yes. And so two Pacific Island Forum nations so far have ratified correct. Palau and FC, That that's correct. And there's a whole bunch of discussions going on amongst other Pacific Island countries in terms of what they need to do to ratify the convention. And, um, uh, we, we expect a number of the other parties to be, to ratify, um, this certainly in the first half of this year.

Thanks. Excellent. Uh, quick question from Dr. Lauren Nan and then honorable Damien O'Connor. Um, Kiara Barry, thank you so much. Um, just a very quick question. I don't know if you heard the previous submitter, um, from MFA as well, but I will be interested to hear if you wouldn't mind elaborating on what do you think, uh, the accountability should be for this particular agreement?

Sorry, the question of accountability? Yes. Oh, maybe I should use this mic. Yeah, Sorry. Um, well, well, the convention, uh, the agreement is, is signed, has a whole bunch of arrangements within it. Um, so there's a accountability mechanisms within the convention and, um, countries.

It will, will basically allow certainly, um, discussions globally on a whole, a range of issues in regard to protection of biodiversity and the scientific and technical body will add to it. And also the way it's been set up, the architecture and how it interacts with, for example, regional fisheries management organizations is, is quite innovative and it will allow for basically dialogue, uh, on a global basis with RFMOs and other agencies.

And I think that's a really important part of the mechanism that's established.
